title: Dimensionality-Driven Electronic and Orbital Transitions Mediating Interfacial Magnetism in LaNiO3/CaMnO3 Observed In Situ
authors: B-A. Courchene, A. Hampel, S. Beck, J. R. Paudel, J. D. Grassi, L. A. Lapinski, A. M. Derrico, M. Terilli, M. Kareev, C. Klewe, A. Gloskovskii, C. Schlueter, S. K. Chaluvadi, F. Mazzola, I. Vobornik, P. Orgiani, J. Chakhalian, A. J. Millis, A. X. Gray
abstract: Emergent magnetic states at oxide interfaces arise from the interplay of charge transfer, orbital reconstruction, and dimensional confinement, offering a route to engineered correlated-electron behavior in nanoscale spintronic materials. Here, we combine in situ synthesis, polarization-dependent angle-resolved photoelectron spectroscopy, X-ray magnetic circular dichroism, and first-principles electronic-structure calculations to investigate LaNiO3/CaMnO3 superlattices. We show that reducing the LaNiO3 thickness drives a metal-insulator transition accompanied by loss of electronic coherence and an orbital-polarization crossover in the ultrathin limit. These changes weaken charge transfer across the interface and suppress the interfacial Mn magnetic moment in CaMnO3, revealing that the emergent ferromagnetic state is directly governed by electronic confinement in LaNiO3. The insulating state and orbital reconstruction are reproduced by density functional theory combined with dynamical mean-field theory. Together, these results establish a direct and tunable coupling among electronic, orbital, and magnetic degrees of freedom in oxide heterostructures.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the emergent ferromagnetism at the LaNiO3/CaMnO3 interface is controlled by the electronic state of the LaNiO3 layer. As the layer is thinned, a metal-insulator transition and orbital reconstruction occur, which shuts down the charge transfer necessary for interfacial magnetism.
main_problem: Investigating how dimensionality-driven electronic and orbital transitions in ultrathin LaNiO3 layers mediate emergent interfacial magnetism in LaNiO3/CaMnO3 superlattices.
main_result: Reducing LaNiO3 thickness drives a metal-insulator transition and an orbital-polarization crossover, which suppresses charge transfer across the interface and subsequently suppresses the interfacial Mn magnetic moment.
method: The study combines in situ pulsed-laser deposition, polarization-dependent ARPES, XMCD, and HAXPES with first-principles DFT+DMFT calculations.
model_or_system: Epitaxial LaNiO3/CaMnO3 superlattices with varying LaNiO3 thicknesses (1, 3, 4, and 6 unit cells) grown on LaAlO3 (001) substrates.
key_observables: Fermi-surface intensity maps, quasiparticle coherence, orbital polarization (dz2 vs dx2-y2), interfacial Mn magnetic moment, and near-Ef spectral weight.
important_parameters: LaNiO3 thickness (1 to 6 unit cells), ultrathin limit, metal-insulator transition (MIT) onset at 3 u.c., and biaxial compressive strain (-1.1%).
main_assumptions: Calculations were performed in the paramagnetic state and used Padé approximants for analytical continuation of self-energy.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the superlattice schematic and experimental geometry; Supplemental figures show structural characterization (XRD, XRR, LEED, STM), chemical composition (HAXPES), and theoretical spectral functions (DFT+DMFT).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of interfacial magnetism, describes the experimental growth and characterization of superlattices, presents ARPES and XMCD results showing the thickness-dependent electronic and magnetic transitions, and concludes with DFT+DMFT validation of the orbital-driven mechanism.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
